name: Polonius
summary: Niko Matsakis's reformulation of Rust borrow-checking as a per-CFG-point loan-liveness problem rather than NLL's region-as-set-of-points model. Origins are sets of loans with subset constraints computed at every CFG point. Original 2018 prototype was Datalog-based; the 2024 Polonius alpha landed in rustc behind -Zpolonius=next as a strict superset of NLL.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Niko Matsakis's **Polonius** is a reformulation of borrow-checking as a per-CFG-point *loan-liveness* problem rather than NLL's region-as-set-of-points model. Origins are sets of *loans* with subset constraints computed at every CFG point, which directly resolves "NLL problem case #3" and lending-iterator patterns (`fn next(&mut self) -> &mut T`) that NLL rejects.

The original 2018 prototype was Datalog-based (the `datafrog` engine, exporting `borrow_region` / `subset_base` / `loan_invalidated_at` facts); the 2024 "Polonius alpha" landed in `rustc` as a location-sensitive analysis behind `-Zpolonius=next` — a strict superset of NLL.

sections.title: Co-development with a-mir-formality
sections.content:
Polonius is co-developed with `a-mir-formality`, a formal model of MIR + the trait system used to derive the location-insensitive subset that ships first. The end-state goal is a single reformulation of the borrow checker that passes more programs and is easier to reason about.

Lending-iterator patterns and method calls that consume self by mutable reference and re-yield a borrow of an internal field are the canonical example of programs Polonius accepts that NLL rejects.
